_provenance: Claude-authored original CET-6 careful reading, batch E3. ~280-340 word academic/social passages, 4 MCQs each (detail/inference/main idea/vocabulary-in-context/attitude). DeepSeek not used.

sets.passage: For many people, the mere prospect of solving a mathematical problem produces a wave of dread. This phenomenon, known as 'mathematics anxiety', is far more than a simple dislike of the subject; it is a genuine emotional response that can interfere with the very ability to think. The mechanism is now reasonably well understood. When an anxious person confronts a maths task, their worry consumes a portion of what psychologists call 'working memory' — the limited mental workspace in which we hold and manipulate information. With part of this capacity occupied by anxiety, less remains available for the calculation itself, and performance suffers. The result is a cruel, self-reinforcing cycle: anxiety causes poor performance, poor performance deepens the anxiety, and a belief in one's own incompetence hardens. Significantly, research indicates that maths anxiety is not the same as a lack of mathematical ability. Many capable people are held back not by any genuine inability, but by the fear itself. The problem, in other words, is often emotional rather than intellectual. This finding points towards solutions. Because the difficulty lies partly in anxiety, addressing the emotion directly can help. Simple techniques, such as briefly writing down one's worries before an exam, have been shown to free up working memory and improve performance. Equally important is challenging the widespread and damaging belief that mathematical ability is a fixed, inborn trait that one either possesses or lacks. The evidence suggests, on the contrary, that ability grows with effort — and that the first step to overcoming maths anxiety may be simply to stop believing that one cannot do it. That single shift in self-belief, modest as it may sound, can break the cruel cycle at its very root, freeing the anxious mind to discover an ability that had in fact been there all along, merely hidden behind the fear.

questions.prompt: What is mathematics anxiety?
options: A simple dislike of the subject.
A genuine emotional response that can interfere with the ability to think.

questions.prompt: How does anxiety harm performance?
options: It consumes working memory, leaving less available for the calculation.
It has no effect at all.

questions.prompt: Is maths anxiety the same as a lack of ability?
options: No; it is often emotional rather than a genuine inability.

questions.prompt: What does the writer suggest as a first step?
options: Stopping the belief that ability is fixed and that one cannot do it.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 干扰思考的真实情绪反应)、细节(B 占用工作记忆、留给计算的更少)、细节(B 常是情绪而非真无能)、主旨(B 第一步是停止'我不行'的信念)。

sets.passage: On a summer night, a city can be several degrees warmer than the countryside that surrounds it. This phenomenon, known as the 'urban heat island', is a direct consequence of how we build our cities, and as the climate warms, it is becoming a matter of serious concern. The causes are straightforward. The dark materials of which cities are largely made — asphalt roads, concrete, and tar roofs — absorb the sun's heat during the day and release it slowly through the night, so that the city never fully cools. The shortage of vegetation removes the natural cooling that plants provide, while waste heat from vehicles, machinery and air conditioners adds further warmth. The consequences are more than a matter of comfort. During heatwaves, the extra warmth of cities can be deadly, particularly for the elderly and the poor, who are least able to escape it. The heat also drives up the use of air conditioning, which consumes energy and, in a vicious circle, releases yet more heat into the urban air. Fortunately, the problem can be eased. Planting trees and creating parks provides shade and natural cooling. Painting roofs white, or covering them with plants, reflects sunlight instead of absorbing it. Such measures are often relatively cheap, and they bring benefits beyond temperature, from cleaner air to more pleasant streets. In a warming world, designing cities to keep cool is no longer a luxury but an increasingly urgent necessity. The cities that learn to stay cool will protect their most vulnerable residents and save a great deal of energy in the bargain, while those that ignore the problem will find their summers growing ever more dangerous as the planet steadily continues to warm.

questions.prompt: What is the 'urban heat island'?
options: The phenomenon of cities being warmer than the surrounding countryside.

questions.prompt: What causes the urban heat island?
options: Dark materials that absorb and slowly release heat, and a shortage of vegetation.

questions.prompt: Why is it more than a matter of comfort?
options: During heatwaves it can be deadly, especially for the elderly and poor.

questions.prompt: How can the problem be eased?
options: It cannot be eased at all.
By planting trees and parks and using reflective or green roofs.
By using far more air conditioning.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 城市比周边乡村更热的现象)、细节(B 深色材料吸放热、缺植被)、细节(B 热浪中对老弱可致命)、主旨(B 植树造园、反光或绿色屋顶可缓解)。

sets.passage: In modern market societies, we are accustomed to exchanging goods for money in transactions that, once completed, leave no further obligation between the parties. Yet anthropologists have long studied a very different system of exchange, the 'gift economy', which operates by an opposite logic and which, surprisingly, still shapes much of our own social lives. In a gift economy, goods circulate not through buying and selling but through giving. Crucially, a gift, unlike a purchase, is never fully 'paid for'. To receive a gift is to incur an obligation, however unspoken, to give something in return at some future time. This unending cycle of giving and reciprocating creates and sustains relationships, binding individuals and groups together in a web of mutual obligation. The point of such gift-giving is often not the goods themselves but the bond they create. Indeed, in many societies, the most prized gifts are those that are passed on rather than kept, their value lying in the relationships they sustain as they move from hand to hand. We may imagine such systems belong only to distant or ancient cultures, but the logic of the gift remains alive among us. We do not, after all, pay our friends for dinner, or charge our families for favours; to do so would be to insult them, to treat a relationship as a mere transaction. In the spaces of our lives we care about most — family, friendship, community — we still, instinctively, live by the logic of the gift rather than the market, sensing that some bonds are cheapened the moment they are priced. The market and the gift, then, are not relics of two different historical eras but two opposing logics that quietly coexist within every modern life, and knowing which of them a given relationship truly calls for is among the subtle arts of living well among others.

questions.prompt: How do goods circulate in a gift economy?
options: Through giving, not buying and selling.
They do not circulate at all.

questions.prompt: What does receiving a gift create?
options: An unspoken obligation to give something in return in the future.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, 'reciprocating' most nearly means
Where does the logic of the gift survive among us?

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 靠赠予流通而非买卖)、细节(B 收礼即负将来回赠之义务)、词义(B reciprocating=回赠)、主旨(B 礼物逻辑仍存于亲情友情社区)。

sets.passage: It was long believed that the adult brain was essentially fixed — that its structure, set in childhood, remained unchanged thereafter, and that we lost the capacity to form new connections as we aged. Over recent decades, this view has been decisively overturned by the discovery of what scientists call 'neuroplasticity': the brain's remarkable ability to reorganise itself throughout life. The evidence is compelling. When people learn a new skill — to play an instrument, or to juggle — the regions of the brain involved measurably grow and strengthen. After an injury such as a stroke, the brain can sometimes reassign functions from damaged areas to healthy ones, allowing lost abilities to be recovered. The very act of thinking and learning, it turns out, physically reshapes the organ that does the thinking. This plasticity is a double-edged sword. The same mechanism that allows us to learn beneficial skills also entrenches harmful habits, carving them ever deeper into our neural pathways through repetition. The brain, in this sense, does not distinguish between good habits and bad; it simply strengthens whatever is practised. The implications are both hopeful and demanding. They are hopeful because they mean we are never simply prisoners of our past or our age; the capacity to change, to learn, to recover, persists throughout life. But they are demanding because they place responsibility upon us: since our brains are shaped by what we repeatedly do and think, we are, in a real and physical sense, continually building the minds we will have to live with. We are, quite literally, the architects of our own brains. Every repeated thought and action, on this view, is a small instruction to the very organ that will have to carry us through the rest of our lives, which is reason enough to choose, with rather more care than we usually do, what it is we practise day after day.

questions.prompt: What was long believed about the adult brain?
options: That it was fixed and unchangeable after childhood.

questions.prompt: What is neuroplasticity?
options: The brain's ability to reorganise itself throughout life.

questions.prompt: Why is plasticity called a 'double-edged sword'?
options: Because it only ever helps us.
Because it also entrenches harmful habits, not just beneficial skills.
Because it does nothing at all.

questions.prompt: What demanding implication does the writer draw?
options: That we cannot change at all.
That we are, in a real sense, the architects of our own brains.
That our habits do not matter.
That age permanently fixes the brain.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 童年后固定不变)、细节(B 大脑终生自我重组的能力)、细节(B 既固化好技能也固化坏习惯)、主旨(B 我们实为自己大脑的建筑师)。

sets.passage: Modern life depends on global supply chains of astonishing complexity. A single product — a phone, a car, a piece of clothing — may contain components from dozens of countries, assembled through a vast, invisible network spanning the globe. For decades, this system delivered ever-cheaper goods with remarkable efficiency. But recent events have exposed a hidden fragility. The efficiency of these supply chains was built on a principle known as 'just-in-time' production, in which companies, to save money, keep almost no spare stock, relying instead on a constant, perfectly timed flow of parts arriving exactly when needed. This works beautifully — until something goes wrong. A natural disaster, a pandemic, or a blocked shipping route can break a single link, and because the system holds so little in reserve, the disruption ripples outward, halting production of finished goods far away. The deeper problem is a tension between efficiency and resilience. A system optimised for efficiency, with no waste and no spare capacity, is by its nature brittle, vulnerable to any unexpected shock. A more resilient system — one that keeps reserves, that has alternative suppliers — is necessarily less efficient, and so more costly, in normal times. For years, the relentless pursuit of efficiency pushed companies towards fragility, the risks hidden until disaster struck. The lesson now being painfully relearned is an old one: that it can be wise to pay a little more in good times to buy security against the bad, and that a system should be judged not only by how well it performs when all goes smoothly, but by how it copes when, inevitably, something does not. A little deliberate redundancy, long dismissed by managers as mere waste, may turn out to be the necessary price of stability in an uncertain world, and the wisest companies are now quietly rediscovering the very old virtue of keeping something in reserve against the day when it is needed.

questions.prompt: What is 'just-in-time' production?
options: Keeping almost no spare stock, relying on a perfectly timed flow of parts.

questions.prompt: What happens when a single link breaks?
options: Because little is held in reserve, the disruption ripples outward, halting production far away.

questions.prompt: What is the deeper tension the passage identifies?
What lesson is being painfully relearned?
options: To maximise efficiency at all costs.
That it can be wise to pay more in good times to buy security against the bad.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 几乎不留库存、靠精准及时的零件流)、细节(B 储备少、中断会向远处扩散)、细节(B 效率与韧性之间的张力)、主旨(B 顺境多花一点买逆境的安全是明智的)。

sets.passage: Imagine two equally careless drivers. Both, distracted for a moment, drift across the road. The first happens to hit and kill a child who was crossing; the second, by pure chance, finds no one there, and arrives home with nothing worse than a fright. The first will be condemned as a killer, perhaps imprisoned, and will be tormented by guilt for the rest of his life; the second will likely forget the incident by morning. Yet the two behaved identically. The only difference between them was luck. This unsettling scenario illustrates what philosophers call 'moral luck': the troubling fact that our moral judgements of people often depend on factors entirely beyond their control. We feel, strongly, that we should be judged only for what is within our power — our choices and intentions, not the random outcomes of chance. And yet, in practice, we judge people heavily by results. The puzzle runs deeper than the law. We praise the lucky entrepreneur whose gamble paid off as 'bold' and condemn the unlucky one whose identical gamble failed as 'reckless', though their decisions, judged in advance, were the same. Whether an action is seen as wisdom or folly often depends on an outcome the actor could not have known. There is no easy resolution. Perhaps the lesson is a measure of humility: to recognise how large a role chance plays in the lives we are quick to judge, including the moral credit we claim for ourselves. Much of what we take pride in, and much of what we condemn in others, may be, in the end, a matter of luck. To hold this uncomfortable thought steadily is not to abolish responsibility altogether, but to temper our harshest judgements with the humbling awareness that, but for a slightly different roll of the dice, we ourselves might very easily have stood in the other person's place.

questions.prompt: What is 'moral luck'?
options: The fact that moral judgements often depend on factors beyond a person's control.

questions.prompt: How do we feel we ought to be judged?
options: Only for our choices and intentions, what is within our power.

questions.prompt: What example beyond the law does the writer give?
options: A 'bold' lucky entrepreneur versus a 'reckless' unlucky one whose decisions were identical.

questions.prompt: What lesson does the writer suggest?
options: To always judge people by results.
A measure of humility about how large a role chance plays.
That we control everything we do.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 道德评判常取决于不可控因素)、细节(B 只应据可控的选择与意图受评)、细节(B 同样决定、运气不同被赞'果敢'或斥'鲁莽')、主旨(B 应对运气之作用保持谦卑)。

sets.passage: The discovery of antibiotics in the twentieth century was one of medicine's greatest triumphs, transforming once-deadly infections into minor inconveniences and making possible much of modern surgery. Yet this triumph is now under threat from a danger that is, ironically, a direct consequence of the drugs' success: antibiotic resistance. The process is a textbook case of evolution in action. Whenever antibiotics are used, the most vulnerable bacteria are killed, but any that happen to carry resistance survive and multiply, passing their resistance on. Over time, and especially where antibiotics are overused, the bacteria as a population become steadily harder to kill. The more we use these drugs, the faster we erode their power. The misuse driving this process takes many forms: antibiotics prescribed for illnesses they cannot cure, courses not completed, and, on a vast scale, the routine feeding of antibiotics to farm animals to promote growth. Meanwhile, the development of new antibiotics has slowed almost to a halt, because such drugs, taken only briefly, are far less profitable for companies than medicines for chronic conditions. The consequences of inaction would be severe: a future in which routine operations become dangerous and minor infections once again kill. Addressing the threat requires action on many fronts — using existing antibiotics far more sparingly, developing new ones, and coordinating globally, since bacteria respect no borders. The antibiotic crisis is a sobering reminder that even our greatest victories over nature may be temporary, and that the power of our medicines is not a permanent possession but something we must actively work to preserve. The drugs that we now take so casually for granted were the hard-won achievement of an earlier and more careful age, and whether they remain effective for our own grandchildren depends entirely on the discipline and the foresight that we are willing to exercise today.

questions.prompt: What now threatens the triumph of antibiotics?
How does resistance develop?
options: Vulnerable bacteria die while resistant ones survive and multiply.

questions.prompt: Why has the development of new antibiotics slowed?
options: Because they are very easy to make.
Because, taken only briefly, they are less profitable than chronic-disease drugs.
Because no one ever gets ill.

questions.prompt: What sobering reminder does the crisis offer?
options: That our victories over nature may be temporary and must be actively preserved.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 抗生素耐药性)、细节(B 易感菌死、耐药菌存活繁殖)、细节(B 用药期短、利润低于慢性病药)、主旨(B 对自然的胜利或为暂时、需主动维护)。

sets.passage: In the age of the internet, nothing is ever truly forgotten. A youthful mistake, an old photograph, a long-regretted comment — all may linger online indefinitely, easily found by anyone who searches. This permanence has given rise to a difficult new question: should people have a 'right to be forgotten', a right to have outdated or embarrassing information about themselves removed from the internet? Those who support such a right argue that human dignity requires the possibility of a fresh start. Throughout history, people have been able to move on from their pasts, to grow and change without being defined forever by an old error. The internet, by remembering everything, threatens this basic human possibility, trapping people permanently in their worst moments. Critics, however, warn of the dangers of allowing information to be erased. A right to be forgotten, they argue, can easily become a tool of censorship — a way for the powerful to hide their misdeeds, for politicians to bury inconvenient truths, or for the rich to scrub their histories clean. The public, they insist, has a legitimate interest in remembering, and the free flow of information is a foundation of an open society. The difficulty is that both sides are partly right. The challenge lies in balancing two genuine and competing goods: the individual's interest in privacy and a second chance, and society's interest in truth, memory and free expression. Where exactly to draw the line — whose past may be forgotten, and whose must be remembered — is among the thorniest questions of the digital age, and one to which there is no simple answer. What is clear, however, is that a society which remembers absolutely everything, forgiving nothing and forgetting no one, may prove every bit as quietly oppressive in its own way as one that allows the powerful to erase whatever they happen to find inconvenient.

questions.prompt: What new question does the internet's permanence raise?
options: Whether people should have a 'right to be forgotten' online.

questions.prompt: What do supporters of the right argue?
options: That information should never be removed.
That human dignity requires the possibility of a fresh start.

questions.prompt: What do critics warn?
options: That it can easily become a tool of censorship for the powerful.

questions.prompt: What is the central difficulty?
options: Balancing the individual's privacy against society's interest in truth and free expression.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 是否应有'被遗忘权')、细节(B 人的尊严需要重新开始的可能)、细节(B 可能沦为权势者的审查工具)、主旨(B 在隐私/重来与真相/言论自由间求平衡)。

sets.passage: A surprisingly large proportion of what we do each day is not the product of conscious decision but of habit — automatic routines triggered by cues in our environment and performed with little thought. Understanding how habits work has practical importance, for it offers a key to changing them. Psychologists describe the habit as a loop with three parts: a cue, a routine, and a reward. A cue — a time of day, a place, an emotional state — triggers a routine, the behaviour itself, which delivers a reward that the brain comes to crave. Repeated often enough, the loop becomes automatic; the cue alone is enough to set the whole sequence in motion, bypassing conscious thought entirely. This structure explains why habits are so hard to break by willpower alone. The routine is bound tightly to its cue and its reward, and simply resolving to stop rarely works for long. A more effective strategy, research suggests, is not to fight the habit head-on but to redirect it: to keep the same cue and the same reward, but to substitute a new, healthier routine in place of the old one. The science of habit carries a paradoxical lesson. On one hand, our automatic behaviours can imprison us, running our lives without our awareness or consent. On the other, the very automaticity that makes bad habits so stubborn can be harnessed for good: by deliberately building beneficial routines until they too become automatic, we can, in effect, programme our future selves to do the right thing without the constant, exhausting effort of conscious will. The most effective path to lasting change, in other words, runs not through heroic and short-lived feats of willpower, but through the patient, almost invisible work of reshaping the small daily routines that, repeated without thought, quietly compose the whole of a life.

questions.prompt: What are habits, according to the passage?
options: Automatic routines triggered by environmental cues.

questions.prompt: What are the three parts of a habit loop?
options: A start, a middle and an end.
A cue, a routine, and a reward.

questions.prompt: What is a more effective strategy than willpower?
options: Keeping the same cue and reward but substituting a new routine.

questions.prompt: What paradoxical lesson does habit science carry?
options: That habits can never be changed.
That the automaticity of habits can imprison us but can also be harnessed for good.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 由环境线索触发的自动routine)、细节(B 线索-routine-奖赏)、细节(B 保留线索与奖赏、替换新routine)、主旨(B 自动性既能困住人也能被善用)。

sets.passage: We usually think of evolution as a biological process, but ideas, too, evolve. Languages, technologies, beliefs and customs change over time through a process that, while not identical to biological evolution, shares some of its deep logic. Understanding this 'cultural evolution' sheds light on why human societies are as they are. Like genes, ideas are copied — passed from person to person, generation to generation — and in the copying, they vary and are selected. Some ideas spread widely while others die out, and which succeed depends not only on how useful or true they are, but on how easily they are remembered, how emotionally appealing they are, and how readily they are transmitted. An idea may flourish, in other words, not because it benefits those who hold it, but simply because it is good at spreading — much as a virus spreads regardless of its effect on its host. This has a sobering implication. We tend to assume that widely held beliefs must contain some wisdom, that their very persistence is evidence of their value. But cultural evolution warns us that this need not be so. A belief may be common not because it is true or useful, but merely because it possesses qualities that help it propagate. Yet cultural evolution also explains humanity's extraordinary success. No individual could invent, from scratch, the vast accumulated knowledge embodied in a modern language, a body of science, or a complex technology. These are the products of countless minds across countless generations, each making small improvements that are passed on and built upon. We are clever, in the end, less as individuals than as participants in a vast, collective process of cultural evolution that no single mind could ever match. Each of us, however ordinary, is at once an heir to this immense inheritance and a small contributor to it, carrying forward the accumulated wisdom of the dead and, just occasionally, adding some tiny improvement of our own for those who will come after us.

questions.prompt: How do ideas evolve, like genes?
options: They are copied, vary, and are selected.
They are invented fresh each time.

questions.prompt: Why might an idea flourish?
options: Only if it is entirely true.
Because it is good at spreading, not necessarily because it benefits those who hold it.

questions.prompt: What sobering implication follows?
options: That common beliefs are always wise.
That a belief may be common not because it is true but because it spreads well.

questions.prompt: Why are humans clever, ultimately?
options: Less as individuals than as participants in a collective process of cultural evolution.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 被复制、变异并被选择)、细节(B 因善于传播而非有益于持有者而盛行)、细节(B 流行的信念未必真或有用)、主旨(B 人之聪明在于参与集体的文化演化)。
